narrative_blocks.header: Drowned in Lake Michigan at forty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet E. Olger Walker drowned at Grand Haven on Lake Michigan on August 15, 1926, at age forty-one. Her sister Hazel had married Harriet's husband's brother fifteen years earlier — two Olger sisters had married two Walker brothers, and both sisters died young.

bio.extra_names: Source records his name as 'FRANCIS SEWELL (HAMILTON) WILKINSON' — born with biological father's surname Hamilton, recorded with Wilkinson at death. Reason for surname change not documented (probable adoption by stepfather, undocumented).

parents.father_research_notes: Robert Addyman Hamilton (b. July 21, 1871 in Aurora Kane County IL; d. January 16, 1945 in Pontiac Oakland County MI; buried Oak Grove Cemetery, Milford Oakland County MI). Biological father, never married Francis's mother Cuma M. Olger (HD3519). Not built as separate entry per Jalapeño Rule — out-of-wedlock partner of a Hooker descendant with no Hooker bloodline of his own.

narrative_blocks.body: Source genealogies record his name as 'Francis Sewell (Hamilton) Wilkinson' — birth surname Hamilton, surname Wilkinson at death. The reason for the change is undocumented but suggests adoption by an unidentified stepfather. His mother Cuma had married William Floyd Welch in 1914 when Francis was six, but he never carried the Welch name.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at sixteen, divorce inferred from timeline
narrative_blocks.body: Beatrice married Samuel Brown on January 5, 1927 in Greenville Michigan — she was sixteen and he was seventeen. By March 1948 she had remarried Leaton Coston while Brown was still living, indicating divorce or legal separation rather than widowhood; Brown did not die until 1966. The Fowler genealogy source does not explicitly document the M1 ending.

narrative_blocks.header: Married on Independence Day at age eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Homer and Hazel Goodwin both eighteen years old married on July 4, 1937 in Greenville Michigan. The couple lived together for 48 years until Homer's death in 1985 at age 65-66. Hazel followed him in 1987; both buried at Sheridan Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Olger sisters married two Walker brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Hazel Olger married Ward Joseph Walker in April 1911. Five months later her sister Harriet married Ward's brother Peter James Walker — two Olger sisters married two Walker brothers, both children of Thomas H. Walker and Olive Treehouse.

parents.mother_research_notes: Hazel Olger Walker (HD3521) died March 1, 1916 in Belding MI when Derrell was eight weeks old. Cause of death not documented in source; the timing suggests possible postpartum complications but this is unconfirmed.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries his father Ward Joseph Walker's first name as middle name.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousin Derrell O Thompson also bore the name Derrell.

narrative_blocks.header: Born two months before his mother's death
narrative_blocks.body: Derrell was born in Belding Michigan on January 3, 1916. His mother Hazel Olger Walker died in the same town eight weeks later on March 1, 1916, at age twenty-four — cause undocumented but the timing is consistent with postpartum complications. He was raised by his father Ward Joseph Walker and lived seventy-one years.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Lawn Cemetery, Saginaw Saginaw County MI.

cross_connections.display_label: Possibly named for his aunt Carroll Olger.

narrative_blocks.body: Carroll and his twin brother Derrell were born on March 6, 1921 in Saginaw Michigan. Derrell died the same day; Carroll lived approximately one day longer, dying March 7. Both were buried at Forest Lawn Cemetery in Saginaw and were the only children their parents had — Elizabeth and Alvin Thompson lived on for forty-one and thirty-nine more years respectively.

narrative_blocks.body: Jack and Evelyn Cornwall married May 5, 1949 in Monroe County Florida — meaning the Florida Keys, far from their Michigan home and unusual for a wedding location in the era. He was 20 and she was 19. The couple lived out their lives in Michigan; both died there in the 2000s.

burial.plot_notes: Oak Grove Cemetery, St. Louis Gratiot County MI. Per Abel Fowler genealogy.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries his maternal grandmother Lucilla Kirtland Gridley's maiden surname as middle name.

narrative_blocks.body: Edward's middle name 'Kirtland' came from his maternal grandmother Lucilla Kirtland Gridley — the same surname his uncle John Kirtland Gridley had inherited as a middle name a generation earlier. The Kirtland surname passed through three Gridley-Fowler generations as a middle name without anyone in the family carrying it as a last name.

burial.plot_notes: Churches Corners Cemetery, Wheatland Township Hillsdale County MI.

parents.father_research_notes: Abel Fowler of Churchville NY. Per EH 1909 entry 2242 and the published Fowler genealogy 'Descendants of Abel Fowler.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Eddy Fowler. Per EH 1909 entry 2242.

narrative_blocks.header: Origin of the name Anson remains uncertain
narrative_blocks.body: Anson was born in Fairfield Herkimer County NY in 1809 to Abel and Mary Eddy Fowler — but the Fowler line shows no documented earlier Anson. Genealogists have suggested the name may trace to Mary's possible Eddy or Ross matrilineal kin: a William Anson Eddy and an Anson Ross were both born around 1809, and three Ross sisters had married three Eddy brothers in that family network. Whether the name came from those collateral relatives or from a popular contemporary figure named Anson is unresolved.

narrative_blocks.header: Frontier child became Michigan land-patent pioneer
narrative_blocks.body: Anson was about seven when his parents moved the family west from Herkimer County NY to Genesee County NY around 1816. After marrying Harriet Louisa Gridley in Ogden NY in 1831, the couple bought land in Wheatland Township Hillsdale County Michigan from a federal land office in 1835 — second-wave New England migration into the Old Northwest. Three of their five surviving children were born in Wheatland on that patent.

sources.label: Melissa Craven Fowler — Fowler family genealogist

burial.plot_notes: Churches Corners Cemetery, Wheatland Township Hillsdale County MI. Per Abel Fowler genealogy.

narrative_blocks.body: Harriet Louisa Gridley and Anson Fowler married at Ogden NY in 1831 and later migrated to Wheatland Township in Hillsdale County Michigan — among the first Hooker-descended families to settle west of New York. Four of their five children were born in Michigan; Anson is buried at Churches Corners Cemetery there.

narrative_blocks.header: Charter member of frontier Free-Will Baptist Church
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet was an early member of the First Free-Will Baptist Church of Wheatland Michigan, organized February 19, 1838 in Eli Eastman's home shortly after the Fowlers arrived. The church gathered at Church's Corners just a few miles from her farm; local accounts noted that before the congregation organized, 'there was more rifle-shooting done on Sunday than any other day.'

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at forty-five, managed her own land deals
narrative_blocks.body: When Anson died in 1855 Harriet was forty-five, with five surviving children. Hillsdale County records show her buying and selling acreage within a few miles of the original 1835 patent for the next two decades — alone or jointly with relatives. She remained financially comfortable through the 1870 and 1880 censuses, outlived Anson by forty-three years, and died in Wheatland Township in 1898.
